paragraphs.body: Nearly causing an elderly woman to fall over on her way in, Eliza rushes out of Costa, trying to balance three coffees in hand with barely enough time to catch the District Line. Turning the corner, she hurries down the stairs into the Underground while rummaging through her knock off Longchamp in search of her oyster card.

annotations.text: elderly woman to fall over on her way in
annotations.comment: Did she run into the woman?
In her hands? In one hand? (If so, why?)
If you’re aiming for London, it’s usually “the Tube” isn’t it? I also assumed that Autumn was American, as “sweet Jesus” isn’t really very British.

paragraphs.body: “I mean sweet Jesus she acts like we can’t get free coffee at our damn workpl--”

“Well good morning to you as well Autumn.” Eliza surprises her and Claire by holding out the carrying tray with the two additional coffees sitting hot and steaming. Sam offers an apologetic laugh and a wry smile. A cool rush of air hits the three of them as the train approaches the platform. The doors open and the three of them shuffle into the small train. Their long, black, wool trench coats bunch together as they squeeze into three available seats.

annotations.comment: The carrying tray seemed a little less cumbersome than what was first described. All of my picky points aside, though, I like this opening — you’ve put us in their morning nicely.
annotations.context_left: “Well good morning to you as well
annotations.context_right: surprises her and Claire by holding out the carrying tray with the two additional coffees sitting ho

paragraphs.body: “Ugh thank you Lizzie, this is gonna be better than the lukewarm river water they have in the office for us anyway.” Claire says excitedly.

annotations.comment: Don’t need the adverb. Again, it’s fine, but they do sound American.

paragraphs.body: “Christ that’s hot!” Sam corrects herself as Claire elbows her “I mean, thank you Liza. What has you running late anyway?”

annotations.comment: How do they know she’s late? She’s on the same train as they are.

paragraphs.body: “I don’t know I woke up late so that already had me frazzled and it takes me a good 10 minutes to walk to the station and the Costa queue was ages long, just one thing after the other.” She sighs.
Claire nudges Eliza, “Maybe it’s nerves eh?”
“Oh yeah, exciting date with the nepo baby right?’ Sam teases.

Eliza blushes as she takes another sip of her coffee. Through a slight laugh she insists, “please just cause he’s a banking nepo baby doesn’t mean he’s not a nice guy. We’ve just chatted a few times in the office and he seems quite genuine.”

annotations.comment: Capitalize and format dialogue correctly throughout. Do they not text with each other beforehand? Were the girls expecting to see her on the train or not?

paragraphs.body: “He looks like every finance bro you and Sam went to LSE with,” Claire jokes. Sam gives a slight nod in agreement. The train begins to screech as it approaches their stop.
Standing up Eliza reassures herself, “Well either way, we’re just going to a pub and it’s only Thursday so nothing too high stakes.”
“Yes yes it’ll be fun Lizzie, you’ll have to keep us updated throughout.” Claire claps her hands in excitement for her friend.
“You know I will and if it all goes to shit I’ll be sure to stage an emergency.”

annotations.comment: What does she mean by this?

paragraphs.body: Twelve Hours Later

Sitting on a particularly uncomfortable bar stool, Adam rolls up the sleeves of his button down and checks the time on his vintage rolex. He’d been in The Fox, a favorite pub of his, for almost 20 minutes now. He was almost about to order his second pint. He thought it rude to continue without his date, but then he hears the large bell above the old wooden door ring back and forth. Stepping through the threshold, Eliza shuts the door swiftly in order to stop the breeze coming in from the Thames nearby. She swings her small purse off her shoulder so she can take off her coat and hang it on a hook by the entrance. Across the pub, Adam’s eyes follow her arms as they slide out of her jacket. Thin, but toned. He likes them that way. A black top hugs her waist. Also thin, but accompanied by good hips. Not too wide. Perfectly suggestive. Eliza turns away from the entrance before glancing around to find Adam. He immediately puts on a warm smile. Before she notices him, she takes in her surroundings. The dim Tiffany style chandelier lights hanging low above the firm wooden tables and the red leather accents of the booths and stools gave it a warm atmosphere. There were a lot of older business looking men, a few women of different ages, but no more than ten. What feels like an eternity later, Adam’s white teeth finally catch Eliza’s attention and she heads in his direction. He stands up to greet her as they say hello. In a quick embrace she takes note of how he towers over her. His cologne smells a little immature for a 25 year old man, but she was willing to look past it as he was so handsome. He has a firm grip on her waist in the hug and as she takes the stool next to him.

annotations.comment: I like the switch in POV, although I didn’t feel like I got to know Eliza so well that it was necessary.
I immediately disliked him for this.
Ah, now I actually hate him. You may want that!

annotations.text: The dim Tiffany style chandelier lights hanging low above the firm wooden tables and the red leather accents of the booths and stools gave it a warm atmosphere. There were a lot of older business looking men, a few women of different ages, but no more than ten.
annotations.comment: You might try putting this more in his POV, as this seems like her less judgmental voice, not his.

annotations.text: immature for a 25 year old man
annotations.comment: Can you make this more specific? Why does she think this? I also wondered why she hasn’t noticed his cologne before, if they work together?

paragraphs.body: Adam calls out her tardiness before handing her a drink menu, “Happy to see you didn’t stand me up, that would’ve been a less than ideal situation for us both.”
Not knowing how genuine his comment was Eliza explains herself, “Oh I know I’m so sorry, the train took longer than I imagined, and I hadn’t ever been here before,”

annotations.text: knowing how genuine his comment was Eliza explains herself, “
annotations.comment: Try to avoid explaining the dialogue before they say it. You might go with, “Taken aback, Eliza says, “Oh,…” here.
annotations.context_right: Oh I know I’m so sor

paragraphs.body: “Oh it’s alright love, I still have a fun night planned for us.” Adam insists. What exactly he means by that, Eliza wasn’t too sure considering she thought it would just be a casual night of drinks and conversation, but she goes along with it anyway. “Well I’m excited for whatever is in store, Adam.”

annotations.comment: Just a note that you don’t need to bend yourself into a pretzel trying to come up with variations for “said.”
Here, you moved us to her POV again. Does she feel okay with this comment? I find it very worrying.

paragraphs.body: Flagging down the bartender, a woman who looked roughly around her age, Eliza orders the pub’s take on an old fashioned. Bourbon, bourbon infused cherry syrup, and bitters of course.

annotations.text: the pub’s take on an old fashioned.
annotations.comment: Do British pubs have table service?

paragraphs.body: Adam seeming slightly taken aback by her choice quips, “Old fashioned eh? Didn’t take a small girl like you for a bourbon drinker.”

annotations.comment: OMG, he’s the worst.

paragraphs.body: In a flirtatious manner she chirps back, “Oh you know, like to try a little bit of everything.” The conversation finally begins to turn into something natural as they talk about their personal lives, their niche interests, how they got their jobs, and their different thoughts on the city. Adam, however, dominates the conversation when it comes to any topic work or university related. He rambles on about the extremely rigorous course load he had in college, the same courses Eliza had, and how they taught him about academic resilience and expanding the mind. Eliza nodded along as if she related to his same experiences. Meanwhile her inner monologue kept asking more and more questions as she took bigger sips of her cocktail. Is this guy serious? What possibly could Advanced Corporate Finance teach him about expanding the mind? That class was dreadful but I can’t say I struggled with it that much? When Adam was just about to unpack his fourth reason he was able to justify his time at LSE even though he was surrounded by “less prestigious” peers the whole time, Eliza interrupts him.

annotations.text: Adam, however, dominates the conversation when it comes to any topic work or university related.
annotations.comment: You might consider showing us a bit more of this dialogue, letting us see this for ourselves. You have a nice ear for it.
BURN

I’m wondering a bit about whether she’s surprised at his behavior. They know each other from work, and she found him so genuine then, you took pains to set up, but now it seems more like a blind date.

paragraphs.body: “I’m just going to use the ladies room really quick. It'll only be a minute.” He nods after a large gulp of his third or fourth pint. As Eliza slides off the tall barstool Adam flags down the bartender. “Yeah she’ll have another.”
In the bathroom, not having to actually pee, Eliza takes her phone out of her back pocket to update her friends.

annotations.comment: We figured this out. :)

paragraphs.body: CLAIRE
How’s it going? Hope you’re having fun! I’ve stalked all of his socials, so sexy.

annotations.text: all of his socials, so sexy
annotations.comment: Again, don’t they all work together? Do all of these folks text with such grammatical correctness?

paragraphs.body: SAM
I told her to quit, she’s gonna scare the man off before we even get the chance to meet him.
ELIZA
Please do scare him off. He might be too much of a finance bro
for me now that I’m having a proper conversation with him.

annotations.comment: Okay, good, this is the first reference that works.

paragraphs.body: SAM
Oh god, do we need to rescue you? I can fake an emergency or something.
ELIZA
Agh, I think I’ll try to stick it out for another hour or so, expect a debrief later. xx

annotations.text: stick it out for another hour or so
annotations.comment: You might tie this back to her feeling that she needs to, since they work together?

paragraphs.body: SAM
Cheers. Godspeed Lizzie.

Stepping out of the stall she approaches her reflection in the mirror reapplying her lip gloss and fluffing her hair before taking a deep breath and heading back out to the bar. Walking around the corner she spots Adam stirring her drink for her. One more drink and I’ll get the hell out of here. Sliding back onto the barstool she takes a swig of her old fashioned. Hm. More bitter than I remember the last one being. She brushes it off and figures the sooner she drinks it the sooner she can go home. Adam, likely being drunk at this point, slides his hand across her back.

annotations.text: her hair before taking a deep breath
annotations.comment: So, in general, the date feels like a bad idea — don’t casually date your colleagues — so you might think about why she now feels she has to make it work?
Oh no! A roofie! Has she never heard not to do this???

paragraphs.body: “Wanna head out after that one?” He has a suggestive look on his face that she at one point probably would have found charming but after tonight finds it almost repulsive. She looks at her watch.

annotations.comment: Again, I think this would work even better for you if she feels somewhat obliged to be there. It’s the thread in the story that makes it all work, but you’re not quite connecting it.

paragraphs.body: “To be entirely honest, I need to be heading home soon, we do sadly have to be in the office tomorrow.” She quickly senses his annoyance. She stalls for a moment as she sips her drink and clears her throat. Wow, this drink must be much stronger than the last. “Uhm, it may be nice to walk by the river though on the way back to the station?” She suggests.

In a somewhat less enthusiastic tone he agrees, “Sure yeah, that’s fine.” He gulps down the rest of his pint and heads to the door. She huffs and chokes down the rest of her cocktail. Standing up, the room seems to spin for a moment. God, there’s no way I’m drunk am I? I can usually handle two or three cocktails. She finds her bearings and gives the bartender a curt nod and a thank you. Her steps feel heavier as she walks to meet Adam at the door. They walk out of the pub and head down the riverwalk. Time seems to have slowed down a bit. She pulls out her phone to access the transit map. Adam dismisses her actions saying he knows the way, but Eliza continues anyway. She’s realized what’s happening to her and what will soon happen if she doesn’t find a way out of it. She heard the stories of it happening to girls in university. She tries her best not to slur her response.

annotations.comment: Hate him.
A potential moment where someone could help her.
annotations.context_right: a curt nod and a tha

annotations.text: realized what’s happening to her and
annotations.comment: Instead of having the narrator tell us this, show how she begins to act. (You do this later on, but it’s pre-empted by the narration here.)

paragraphs.body: “Oh I do as well I ju- just always like to double check the arrival ti- times.” She feels his large hand gripping tighter on her waist. Unfortunately it’s doing a better job keeping her standing than her own legs. As subtle as possible, she sends an SOS text to her friends and forwards them a pin. Adam notices her small struggles. When she slightly stumbles he plays innocent.

annotations.comment: Can you explain how she does this? Does she type SOS? Is this a plan that she has set up with these girls ahead of time? Is it possible that one or more of them has already been suspicious of him?

paragraphs.body: “Woah there, that bourbon catching up to you huh? Why don’t we sit for a second and take the night in?” he suggests.

Unable to resist his overbearing hold on her, Eliza finds herself sitting on a bench by what she thinks is the part of the riverwalk that goes under a small bridge. She hears Adam say something to her, but can’t make out exactly what. All she knows is she’s feeling weaker by the second. His face is too close to hers, his breath hot and pungent. She has to act now. Mustering any and all strength she can she manages to stagger off of the bench. Adams grip moves to her wrist trying to yank her back. He spits out another comment. It doesn’t make sense to her. She tries to swat his grip away but her heels betray her balance as she hits the cold cobblestone. Adam goes to pick her up as she scoots toward the small ledge that lines the riverwalk. On her way up she feels around her and grabs her heel as tight as she can. Adam’s drunken voice finds its way back into her ear, his hands hurt wrapped around her thin waist, his facial hair stings rubbing up against her neck and shoulder. In a final attempt to get herself out of this, she reaches her arm above the two of them, high heel in hand, before slamming it into the back of his heavy head. He falters a little bit before bringing his hand to the back of his head. He gives Eliza a look of pure rage when he sees the dark blood on his hand. Before he has the ability to hurt her even more in return, someone grabs the heel out of Eliza’s weak hand. With another aggressive bash to the back of the head, Adam’s whole body slumps over the riverwalk wall. He can hardly move. With the third bludgeon, a crack is heard. The weight of Adam’s upper body continues to slide over the wall until another set of hands throw his feet the rest of the way over. A splash is heard, and then nothing but heavy breathing and the rush of the Thames.

annotations.comment: Who rescued her?Elizabeth, this is a great first draft. You told a tight, gripping story and I was interested throughout. Choosing to end with the guy’s (apparent) death is a big swerve and felt a little too obvious to me. (And I also wondered if you could really kill someone by hitting them with a heel?) I wondered how this was all going to play out for her in the next few days, especially given that he’s a coworker. I also assumed that she blacked out? Your opening is weak, which makes sense as I’m sure you were just trying to figure out what story to tell. But you waste a lot of pages on introducing characters we didn’t need to know, and don’t mention some key points, like Eliza’s heels. I also wonder if you really want Adam to be a colleague? You don’t really bring it up again (they could share similar jobs in other companies) and I think it somewhat confuses the story a bit. I enjoyed the hints that she was aware of what was happening and was struggling. They made more sense to me than some of her dumber decisions, like leaving her drink at the table and even going back to the date which was going so badly. Maybe you could think about what pressures he exerts that make her continue against her better judgement? In other words, how does she fight against what’s happening up to the very end?Nice work! Happy revising!
